title: Merger Control ‚Äî Penalties & Procedures
content:
Session 8: Merger review continued [¬ß¬ß 43A, 44 and 45 of the Competition Act  2002; Combination 
regulations 9(4)]  
Proceedings against Amazon.com NV Investment Holdings LLC [2021 | CCI] [2022 | NCLAT] 
Facts:  
‚ùñ‚Äã Amazon ‚Äì FCPL ‚Äì FRL [where, FCPL is a subsidiary of FRL] 
‚ùñ‚Äã Three transactions were notified before the CCI: 
‚û¢‚Äã Issue of Class A voting equity shares of FCPL to Future Coupons Resources Private Limited (FCRPL) 
(Transaction I);  
‚û¢‚Äã Transfer of shares of FRL held by FCRPL representing 2.52% of the issued, subscribed and paid-up equity share 
capital of Future Retail Limited (FRL), on a fully diluted basis to FCPL (Transaction II) and;  
‚û¢‚Äã Acquisition by Amazon of the Subscription Shares representing 49% of the total issued, subscribed and paid-up 
equity share capital of FCPL (on a Fully Diluted Basis) (Transaction III).  
‚ùñ‚Äã BUT prior to Amazon‚Äôs acquisition of shares, FCPL had acquired equity warrants of FRL, convertible into equity shares 
representing 7.30% of the share capital of FRL (Warrants Transaction) which was approved by the CCI through its order 
dated 15.04.2019.  
‚û¢‚Äã Interestingly, the FCPL SHA included the requirement of prior written consent of Amazon for FCPL to decide on 
or implement any matter under FRL SHA.  
‚û¢‚Äã Moreover, several commercial agreements (BCAs) were executed between Amazon and the Future Group to 
consolidate Amazon‚Äôs presence in the retail market.  
‚ùñ‚Äã However, neither the FRL SHA nor the BCAs were notified, as Amazon argued they were not independent of the 
combination. 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Item 5.3 of Form I: The notifying party is required to disclose the economic and strategic purpose of the combination 
under this head.  
147 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Item 8.8 of Form I: The notifying party is to furnish documents and material considered by or presented to the board of 
directors or key managerial person of the parties to the combination or their relevant group entities, in relation to the 
proposed combination.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Regulation 9(4) read with Items 5.1.1, 5.1.2, 5.2 of Form I: This provision makes it mandatory for parties to the 
combination to give a notice covering all inter-connected steps of their proposed combination (being disclosures to be 
made against item 5.1.2).  Moreover, item 5.2 requires the timelines of each inter-connected step to be disclosed.  
 
Application by CCI: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Internal Correspondence 
‚û¢‚Äã Firstly, the internal note dated 24 May 2018 showed that the investment would help Amazon 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Expand coverage in top four cities and improve the merchant fee to 13.5%;  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Build a two-hour-delivery service in the next 20 cities;  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Exclusively carry their private label portfolio in grocery and value-fashion and; 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Obtain option value to increase its equity stake when laws change. 
‚û¢‚Äã Secondly, the situation update dated 10 July 2018 elaborated the background and purpose with which the 
combination was contemplated between Amazon and Future Group.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Amazon viewed the arrangement as a means to utilize the FRL‚Äôs pan-India store infrastructure to boost 
Amazon‚Äôs ultra-fast delivery program, exclusively carry private label portfolios in grocery and value 
fashion (where Amazon was allegedly lagging behind its competitors), and drive higher fees for Amazon. 
‚û¢‚Äã Thirdly, an internal e-mail dated 18 July 2019 demonstrated that in light of the FDI restrictions, Amazon would 
use a twin-entity investment structure to invest in FRL. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
BASICALLY, Amazon would acquire 49% shareholding in FCPL, which in turn would hold 8 to 10% of 
the shareholding in FRL.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Lastly, it also listed out several benefits provided by FRL to Amazon in pursuance to the BCAs. 
‚ùñ‚Äã False, incorrect and incomplete disclosure of information 
‚û¢‚Äã The internal correspondence clearly highlights how the true purpose of the combination was concealed. 
‚û¢‚Äã Amazon had merely submitted a presentation highlighting the gift voucher business of FCPL, its business 
operating model, estimated five-year business size, organisation design, sales team and financial summary, without 
any reference to FRL. In fact, the entire internal correspondence was not disclosed and was actively concealed. 
‚û¢‚Äã Amazon had only identified three transactions and the FCPL SHA and FCPL SSA as agreements that formed a 
part of the combination. The FRL SHA and BCAs were not disclosed against any of the abovementioned items. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Suspension 
‚û¢‚Äã Amazon failed to notify FRL SHA and the BCAs as parts of the Combination between the parties, and suppressed 
the actual purpose and particulars of the combination, in contravention of the obligation contained in subsection 
(2) of section 6 of the Act read with regulation 9(4) of the Combination Regulations. 
‚û¢‚Äã In exercise of the powers conferred under section 45(2) of the Act, the CCI directed Amazon to give notice in Form 
II within a period of 60 days, and, till disposal of such notice, the approval granted to the combination ‚Äòshall 
remain in abeyance‚Äô. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Penalty 
‚û¢‚Äã Penalty of INR 202 crores under section 43-A of the Act.  
‚û¢‚Äã Moreover, in the absence of any mitigating factor, the CCI imposed the maximum penalty of INR 1 crore under 
sections 44 and 45 of the Act respectively, thereby, collectively amounting to a penalty of INR 202 crores. 
 
Application by the NCLAT: Upheld the order of CCI. 
 
 
SCM Solifert v CCI [2018 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
148 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã On July 3, 2013, SCM along with DFPCL had purchased 24.46% of the share capital of MCFL on the BSE (‚ÄòFirst 
Acquisition‚Äô) ‚Üí press release on the same day by DFPCL in compliance with the requirements of the Listing Agreement. 
‚ùñ‚Äã On April 23, 2014, SCM and DFPCL made a purchase in the open market for 0.8% of the shares of MCFL (‚ÄòSecond 
Acquisition‚Äô) and subsequently, an open offer was made for acquiring up to 26% of the shares of MCFL in terms of the SEBI 
SAST Reg.  
‚ùñ‚Äã SCM filed a notice disclosing details of the First Acquisition and notifying the Second Acquisition within 30 days of the 
Public Announcement.  
‚ùñ‚Äã CCI passed an order approving the proposed combination ‚Äì BUT ‚Äì because SCI and DFPL did not seek approval prior to 
consummating the First Acquisition, CCI commenced proceedings for filing the belated notice.  
‚ùñ‚Äã In this regard, SCM claimed that the First Acquisition was exempt, under Schedule 1 of the CCI (Procedure in regard to the 
Transaction of Business relating to Combinations) Regulations, 2011.  
‚û¢‚Äã SCM argued that the First Acquisition was as an acquisition of shares less than 25% in MCFL solely as an 
investment.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
On this basis, SCM assumed that the First Acquisition would qualify for the Item 1 exemption. 
‚û¢‚Äã In any event, SCM submitted that a notice was filed within the 30 days of the Public Announcement made 
pursuant to the Second Acquisition.  
‚û¢‚Äã With regard to the Second Acquisition, the shares were credited to an escrow account, created specifically to avoid 
consummation prior to CCI approval.  
 
Rule: 
 
 
Application: 
149 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã CCI dismissed SCM‚Äôs submissions after noting that the First Acquisition was notifiable ‚Üí the acquisition of shares was not 
made solely as an investment.  
‚û¢‚Äã By its own admission, SCM in the Press Release and the Public Announcement had claimed that the investment 
was ‚Äòvery strategic‚Äô.  
‚û¢‚Äã ALSO ‚Üí MCFL was not very profitable, which further went to show that the First Acquisition could not be 
considered to be an investment by a prudent investor.  
‚ùñ‚Äã SC while agreeing with CCI‚Äôs rationale held that SCM had not complied with Section 6(2) and there was a failure to file 
notice for the First Acquisition.  
‚ùñ‚Äã In relation to the Second Acquisition, SCM ought to have notified the transaction prior to the acquisition and rejected 
SCM‚Äôs claim that it was enough to hold the shares in an escrow account, which could not be accessed prior to CCI approval.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Competition Act does not contemplate post facto notice ‚Üí would defeat CCI‚Äôs opportunity to assess whether the proposed 
combination could cause any appreciable adverse effect on competition.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Accordingly, SCM was required to notify the proposed combination without giving effect to it prior to CCI approval or the 
end of the 210-day period, whichever is earlier.  
‚ùñ‚Äã CCI had already imposed a nominal penalty of ‚Çπ 2 crore (approx. US$ 0.3 million) (even though it had the discretion to go 
up to 1% of the value of the transaction, which would have been ‚Çπ 33.22 crore (approx. US$ 5 million) in the instant case).  
‚û¢‚Äã SC further observed that CCI approval of a transaction cannot by itself condone gun-jumping violations.  
‚û¢‚Äã CCI was not required to demonstrate mens rea or intentional breach as an essential element for imposing penalty 
as this was not a requirement in case of violation of the civil statutory provision.  
‚û¢‚Äã On this basis, SC concluded that the order passed by CCI as affirmed by COMPAT, is in accordance with the law 
and dismissed SCM‚Äôs appeal as being devoid of any merit.
